title: Research Tools Overview
text:
This overview groups the live and planned tools that support CO-LUMINATE data collection, scoring, dashboard interpretation, and causal mapping.





Developmental Cascade Explorer



Six-module interactive dashboard for the developmental cascade framework, from childhood adversity through mediation to policy simulation.

In progress, content not yet published to the public website





IRT Scoring Workspace



Operational scoring tool for SSQ responses, supporting single-case and batch workflows through the project API.

In progress, content not yet published to the public website





Causal Chains of Mental Health



Interactive tool for capturing and reviewing youth-informed causal pathways between lived experience and mental health outcomes.

In progress, content not yet published to the public website

title: Mental Health Causal Chains
text:
This app is intended to help Youth Co-Creators and researchers capture how social, economic, environmental, and relational experiences turn into mental health outcomes.
Instead of storing isolated answers, the app is designed to capture short causal chains such as:
Poverty -> inability to afford food -> stress and worry -> Poor mental health
It supports both harmful and protective pathways so that Positive mental health can be documented alongside Poor mental health.

title: Mental Health Causal Chains
text:
Pathway Inventory

1. Socioeconomic Position, Poverty, and Economic Inequality

Poverty -> inability to afford food -> stress and worry -> mental health decline -> Poor mental health.
Reliance on subsistence farming -> loss of income due to theft or natural hazards -> household chaos -> anger and domestic violence -> negative mental health outcomes -> Poor mental health.
Poverty -> inability to fix or improve living conditions independently -> anxiety and frustration -> Poor mental health.
Hope for the future -> encouragement to persist -> reduced hopelessness -> positive outlook -> improved mental health -> Positive mental health.



2. Education, Employment, and Working Conditions

Lack of school fencing and monitoring -> truancy and time in unsafe spaces -> substance use and sexual activity -> emotional strain and disrupted education -> Poor mental health.
Physical punishment at school -> humiliation -> suppressed emotions -> anger and resentment toward authority -> Poor mental health.
Employment through mining -> financial security -> improved mental health -> Positive mental health.
Mine closure -> loss of income -> reliance on grants -> communal stress and anxiety -> Poor mental health.
Financial stress -> emotional strain -> substance use -> family conflict -> emotional distress -> Poor mental health.
Protracted unemployment -> eroded self-esteem -> stress -> Poor mental health.
Reliance on government grant -> eroded self-esteem -> Poor mental health.



3. Housing and Living Conditions

Lack of running water -> inability to maintain hygiene -> bullying and shame -> emotional distress -> Poor mental health.
Poor condition of houses -> discomfort at home -> mental strain -> Poor mental health.



4. Early Childhood Development

Access to early learning centers -> better performance in higher grades -> increased self-esteem and reduced stress -> Positive mental health.
Access to early learning centers -> safety -> less exposure to trauma -> Positive mental health.



5. Social Support, Social Capital, Discrimination, and Social Exclusion

Migration for work -> family separation -> loneliness and insecurity -> increased stress and anxiety -> Poor mental health.
Church counselling and spiritual guidance -> sense of hope and peace -> Positive mental health.
Access to sports grounds -> social engagement through sport -> reduced substance abuse -> improved mental health -> Positive mental health.
Association with bad peers -> parental disapproval -> conflict -> emotional strain -> Poor mental health.
Feeling excluded from family decision-making -> feelings of marginalisation and powerlessness -> Poor mental health.
Strong family and community social capital -> access to shared resources such as land, gardens, and employment opportunities -> material security and social support -> improved well-being -> Positive mental health.
Low social connectedness and weak community cohesion -> exclusion from shared resources and fear of stigma -> reduced help-seeking and exposure to harm such as abuse or conflict -> stress and emotional distress -> Poor mental health.



6. Physical Environment

Air and water pollution -> ill health -> fear and worry -> Poor mental health.
Mines -> land damage -> grief and stress -> mental health deterioration -> Poor mental health.
Litter from dumping sites -> perceived danger -> avoidance of nature -> reduced relaxation and well-being -> Poor mental health.
Subsistence agriculture -> lower financial pressure -> Positive mental health.
Extreme weather events -> soil erosion and crop destruction -> loss of food and income -> mental distress -> Poor mental health.
Water scarcity -> dual use of water such as drinking water for cattle versus watering crops -> internal conflict -> emotional distress -> Poor mental health.



7. Access to Basic Services

Neglected public infrastructure -> exposure to physical danger -> persistent worry and compensatory costs -> Poor mental health.
Neglected public infrastructure -> inability to use services -> missed opportunities -> frustration -> Poor mental health.
Low political will to improve basic services -> unmet basic needs -> sense of injustice -> emotional distress -> Poor mental health.
Improved health awareness -> lower stigma -> easier access to treatment -> emotional relief and improved physical health -> Positive mental health.
Long queues at clinics -> delays in or avoidance of care -> increased anxiety and discouragement -> Poor mental health.
Stigmatising treatment from healthcare workers -> humiliation -> avoidance of treatment -> worsening health and mental strain -> Poor mental health.
Lack of nearby hospital -> lengthy travel time and transport poverty -> hinders urgent and intensive medical care -> sense of distance from needed care -> Poor mental health.
Domestic violence -> women withdraw cases due to financial dependence -> ongoing distress -> Poor mental health.
Neglected public infrastructure -> unable to cross bridges during floods -> unable to attend school or work -> negatively impacts education and income -> Poor mental health.



8. Violence, Crime, and Safety

Abandoned buildings and taverns -> drug and alcohol use -> increased violence, crime, and risky behaviours -> emotional distress and insecurity -> Poor mental health.
Drug and alcohol use -> domestic violence -> Poor mental health.
Drug and alcohol use -> misuse of income -> debt and poverty -> Poor mental health.



9. Culture, Religion, and Spirituality

Church attendance and pastoral counselling -> support, mediation, and guidance -> Positive mental health.
Prayer and rituals -> spiritual connection and perceived ancestral support -> relief and hope -> Positive mental health.
Fear of witchcraft -> worry and distrust -> mental distress -> Poor mental health.
Dual belief systems -> conflicting norms -> internal conflict -> emotional strain -> Poor mental health.
Rejection by church when engaged in stigmatised behaviour -> loss of status -> feeling shunned -> emotional distress -> Poor mental health.

title: Statistical Analysis Plan
text:
This document is the integrated CO-LUMINATE Statistical Analysis Plan (SAP). It retains the published public section structure while updating the underlying analytical description to reflect the current harmonization, trajectory, and pathway-screening workflows carried forward into the release-safe script bundles.
The current release includes:

Section A: Objective 2 (Depression Harmonization)
Section B: Objective 3 (Social Determinant Trajectories)
Section C: Objective 4 (Behavioral Mediators)

Section D (Objective 5: Causal Mediation) remains an intentional placeholder pending finalization of prespecified mediation model families and implementation diagnostics.

title: Statistical Analysis Plan
section: Section A: Objective 2 - Depression Harmonization SAP
text:
Section A: Objective 2 - Depression Harmonization SAP

A1 Background and Rationale
This SAP component defines the psychometric protocol for harmonizing depression measurement between the Shona Symptom Questionnaire and the Patient Health Questionnaire-9. The central challenge is not simple score conversion, but defensible alignment of instruments that differ in item format, recall period, and cultural grounding in a longitudinal multi-cohort setting.
The PHQ-9 is treated as the external benchmark for probable depression (Kroenke, Spitzer, and Williams 2001), while the SSQ contributes culturally grounded distress content that is relevant in Southern African settings (Patel et al. 1997; Haney et al. 2014; Chibanda et al. 2019). The revised Objective 2 workflow therefore treats harmonization as a staged psychometric program: item-set refinement, structural validation, invariance assessment, joint IRT calibration, and threshold comparison. In the current implementation, the theoretically derived SSQ-10 subset is retained as the main transportable SSQ configuration because it preserves the depressive core while avoiding non-specific distress items that weaken comparability with the PHQ benchmark (Putnick and Bornstein 2016; Woods 2009).


A3 Objectives and Estimands
Let \(Y_i^{PHQ} = \mathbb{1}\{PHQ9_i \ge 10\}\) denote the PHQ-referenced probable-depression indicator for participant \(i\).
The primary estimand is the harmonized binary endpoint
\[
Y_i^{H} = \mathbb{1}\{\hat{\theta}_i \ge \tau^\ast\},
\]
where \(\hat{\theta}_i\) is the calibrated latent depression score from joint PHQ-SSQ modeling and \(\tau^\ast\) is the selected latent threshold linked to the PHQ-9 reference scale.
Secondary estimands include prevalence alignment and threshold transportability:
\[
\Delta_p = \Pr(Y_i^{H}=1) - \Pr(Y_i^{PHQ}=1),
\]
where \(\Delta_p\) summarizes endpoint-prevalence drift under candidate thresholding strategies. In the revised Objective 2 workflow, threshold comparison is not limited to a single rule. The anchored threshold linked to a PHQ-9 score of approximately 10 is compared with ROC-derived operating points and sensitivity- or specificity-targeted alternatives before a transport rule is selected.


A4 Study Design and Analytic Samples
The development sample comprises participants aged approximately 16-24 years with concurrent SSQ and PHQ observations available for joint calibration. The transport sample consists of SSQ-only records used to extend the harmonized scoring rule beyond the concurrent benchmark subset.
Eligibility is stage-specific:

structural-validity stages require complete item vectors for the candidate item set under evaluation;
IRT calibration requires valid item responses under the retained instrument configuration;
threshold comparison requires benchmark PHQ information;
endpoint assignment requires valid latent-score estimates from the final joint or fixed-parameter SSQ scoring workflow.



A5 Structural Modeling Strategy
The revised Objective 2 workflow evaluates three candidate SSQ configurations: SSQ-14, the theoretical SSQ-10 depressive subset, and the SSQ-8 comparison subset (Chibanda et al. 2019). Structural validity begins with Monte Carlo split-sample EFA using polychoric correlation structures and categorical-data retention criteria, followed by hold-out CFA using WLSMV estimation (Holgado–Tello et al. 2010; Wu and Estabrook 2016). Consensus item structures are identified across repeated split samples rather than from a single exploratory fit.
Let \(\mathbf{x}_i\) denote the ordinal or binary symptom vector and \(\theta_i\) the latent depression factor. The CFA measurement model is
\[
\mathbf{x}_i \sim f(\theta_i; \boldsymbol{\lambda}, \boldsymbol{\tau}),
\]
where \(\boldsymbol{\lambda}\) are factor-loading parameters and \(\boldsymbol{\tau}\) are item-threshold parameters. Fit diagnostics are interpreted jointly rather than through single-cutoff rules, consistent with the revised psychometric workflow (Hu and Bentler 1999; MacCallum et al. 2002). Measurement invariance is then assessed across sex and age strata so that downstream thresholding does not rest on item structures that vary materially across key subgroups (Putnick and Bornstein 2016; Vandenberg and Lance 2000).


A6 IRT Harmonization and Threshold Derivation
Harmonization uses graded response models implemented in mirt (Samejima 1969; Chalmers 2012). For item \(j\) and category threshold \(k\),
\[
\Pr(X_{ij} \ge k \mid \theta_i) =
\operatorname{logit}^{-1}\left[a_j(\theta_i - b_{jk})\right].
\]
Separate instrument-level GRMs are used to confirm single-scale adequacy, after which a joint PHQ-SSQ calibration places both instruments on a common latent continuum. Latent trait scores are estimated using expected a posteriori scoring, and SSQ parameters from the joint model are exported as a fixed-parameter scoring engine so that SSQ-only records can be scored without refitting the full calibration model.
The primary applied threshold remains a clinically anchored \(\theta\) cutoff tied to the PHQ-9 test characteristic curve at approximately 10. However, the revised Objective 2 work compares this anchored rule with Youden-based ROC rules and constrained operating rules targeting high sensitivity or specificity before selecting the transportable endpoint definition for downstream Objectives 3-5.


A7 Performance Metrics and Uncertainty
Endpoint performance is summarized by:

sensitivity, specificity, PPV, NPV, and accuracy;
AUC and Cohen’s \(\kappa\);
prevalence drift \(\Delta_p\);
subgroup consistency under alternate threshold strategies.

The current workflow treats discrimination, calibration, and prevalence alignment as a joint decision problem rather than selecting thresholds on AUC or Youden performance alone. Uncertainty is summarized using resampling-based intervals and repeated-split diagnostics where appropriate (Efron and Tibshirani 1994).


A8 Planned Outputs and Reporting
Planned outputs are:

item-retention and structural-validity diagnostics for candidate SSQ configurations;
CFA, invariance, and DIF summaries for the retained SSQ-based depression structure;
joint IRT calibration outputs and threshold-mapping comparisons;
a fixed-parameter SSQ scoring engine for SSQ-only cohorts;
the final harmonized depression endpoint carried forward into downstream Objectives 3-5.

title: Statistical Analysis Plan
section: Section B: Objective 3 - Social Determinant Trajectories SAP
text:
Section B: Objective 3 - Social Determinant Trajectories SAP

B1 Background and Rationale
Objective 3 models developmental trajectories of childhood social determinants and evaluates how those trajectories relate to the harmonized depression endpoint. This SAP treats trajectory modeling as a defensibility problem in which class structure, overlap, and interpretability will be examined across multiple analytic restrictions rather than inferred from a single retained solution (Andruff et al. 2009; D. S. Nagin 2005; Bengt O. Muthén 2001).
This framing follows the current harmonized trajectory workflow, which treats childhood social determinants as time-varying developmental processes and organizes them into early-childhood (0-5 years) and middle-late-childhood (6-12 years) windows (Hall and Posel 2019; Isabel Fortier et al. 2017; Simelane et al. 2025). The scientific aim is to preserve developmental timing while distinguishing robust trajectory structures from those that change materially when stronger repeated-measures requirements are imposed.


B3 Objectives and Estimands
Let \(E_{it}\) denote exposure profile at developmental time \(t\), and let \(C_i\) denote latent trajectory class membership.
Primary estimands include the class-proportion vector
\[
\pi_c = \Pr(C_i = c), \quad c = 1,\dots,K,
\]
and class-conditional depression-risk contrasts
\[
\text{OR}_c =
\frac{\Pr(Y_i^H=1 \mid C_i=c)/\Pr(Y_i^H=0 \mid C_i=c)}
     {\Pr(Y_i^H=1 \mid C_i=c_{ref})/\Pr(Y_i^H=0 \mid C_i=c_{ref})}.
\]
Secondary estimands include posterior classification quality, same-label retention across analytic branches, and cross-scenario reallocation of class membership. These secondary estimands are defined because the Objective 3 workflow will compare unrestricted, primary restricted, sensitivity, and strict joint branches rather than relying on a single fitted branch as the sole evidence source.


B4 Exposure Definitions and Time Windows
Exposure histories are organized into two pre-specified developmental windows: 0-5 years and 6-12 years. All trajectory models are built on harmonized repeated childhood measures, with emphasis on preserving developmental ordering and avoiding post hoc redefinition of windows or determinant families.
The current active Objective 3 model family set is:

ULWOM: univariate maternal co-residence trajectories;
UHSES: univariate household socioeconomic status trajectories;
JPA: joint parental residence pattern trajectories;
JMAES: joint maternal absence plus household socioeconomic status trajectories.

These four families supersede the earlier, narrower public summary and define the current SAP implementation target. Retired or exploratory specifications are excluded from active SAP compliance checks unless they are reintroduced explicitly in a later release.


B5 LCGA and Joint-Trajectory Strategy
The Objective 3 workflow implements a five-branch comparison framework:

unrestricted univariate analyses to maximize sample size under likelihood-based handling of incomplete repeated measures;
primary univariate analyses requiring at least three occasions per window;
sensitivity univariate analyses requiring at least two occasions per window;
main joint analyses requiring at least three occasions;
strict joint sensitivity analyses requiring at least three complete joint occasions.

For participant \(i\) in class \(c\), the class-specific growth model is
\[
E_{it} = \eta_{0c} + \eta_{1c}t + \eta_{2c}t^2 + \varepsilon_{it},
\quad \varepsilon_{it} \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_c^2).
\]
Class-membership probabilities are represented as
\[
\Pr(C_i=c) = \frac{\exp(\alpha_c)}{\sum_{h=1}^{K}\exp(\alpha_h)}.
\]
In practice, the workflow emphasizes common orchestration for model fitting, labeling, plotting, reporting, and audit generation rather than isolated solution fitting (Bengt O. Muthén 2004; Daniel S. Nagin et al. 2016).


B6 Class Enumeration and Model Selection
Class enumeration combines statistical and scientific criteria:

information criteria and neighboring class-count stability;
minimum useful class size and posterior separation;
substantive interpretability of class shapes;
consistency of retained solutions across analytic restrictions;
documentation against GRoLTS and SMART-LCA-aligned reporting standards (Nylund, Asparouhov, and Muthén 2007; Masyn 2013; Lissa, Garnier-Villarreal, and Anadria 2024).

Because mixture solutions are sensitive to specification, local maxima, and sample restriction, retained solutions are also evaluated under structural sensitivity checks and overlap/reallocation reviews (Bauer and Curran 2003; Bauer 2007; Hipp and Bauer 2006; Watson et al. 2022).


B7 Association Modeling and Robustness
Downstream association models condition on the harmonized endpoint \(Y_i^H\) and fixed covariates once selected class structures are carried forward:
\[
\operatorname{logit}\Pr(Y_i^H=1) = \beta_0 + \sum_{c \ne c_{ref}}\beta_c\mathbb{1}(C_i=c) + \boldsymbol{\gamma}^\top\mathbf{Z}_i.
\]
For this SAP, robustness is defined more broadly than the final regression stage alone. Planned robustness evidence includes:

class-count stability across unrestricted and restricted branches;
entropy and posterior-probability comparisons;
same-label retention in overlap samples;
strict-joint boundary-condition checks;
complete audit artifact coverage across all active branches.

This means the SAP treats defensibility and class reallocation as part of the inferential workflow rather than as a post hoc appendix to a single retained model (Schafer and Graham 2002; Graham 2009; Parsons, McCormick, et al. 2024).


B8 Planned Outputs and Reporting
Planned outputs include:

trajectory-profile plots for each active model family and developmental window;
class-enumeration summaries and posterior-quality diagnostics;
overlap and reallocation summaries across unrestricted and restricted branches;
structural-sensitivity and reporting-checklist artifacts;
downstream class-linked depression association tables using the harmonized endpoint.

The public trajectory bundle is specified to use repo-local handoff inputs under data/inputs/ by default, with optional overrides through COLU_CHILDHOOD_RDATA and COLU_MULTISTUDIES_RDATA. This replaces the older private-data path pattern and is the public-facing contract assumed by the current SAP crosswalk.


B10 Consistency Stamp (Objective 3)
Consistency stamp date: 2026-04-09
Scope: Objective 3 (Social Determinant Trajectories) only.
This consistency stamp records the intended SAP scope for the current Objective 3 specification:

the active Objective 3 family set is constrained to ULWOM, UHSES, JPA, and JMAES;
the SAP is aligned to the five-branch defensibility workflow rather than to a single retained LCGA branch;
overlap, entropy, and class-reallocation diagnostics are defined as core implementation evidence;
any future expansion beyond this family set will require an explicit SAP revision.

title: Statistical Analysis Plan
section: Section C: Objective 4 - Behavioral Mediators SAP
text:
Section C: Objective 4 - Behavioral Mediators SAP

C1 Background and Rationale
Objective 4 identifies adolescent behavioral and proximal social pathway variables through which childhood social determinant trajectories may influence depression risk. This SAP defines Objective 4 as a pathway-screening and prioritization analysis, not a formal causal mediation decomposition. This distinction matters in a harmonized multi-cohort platform where pathway variables, endpoint timing, and contextual exposure history all vary across source studies (Jörns-Presentati et al. 2021; Jakobsson et al. 2024; Rossouw et al. 2024).
The current Objective 4 framing treats the adolescent pathway block as a constrained but policy-relevant subset of a broader locally grounded pathway ecology. Candidate domains include material deprivation, violence, school-related adversity, social connectedness, and sexual-risk behavior, all positioned after childhood exposure windows and before the harmonized depressive-symptom endpoint (M. Mkhize, Westhuizen, and Sorsdahl 2024; Partap et al. 2023; Zimmerman et al. 2021).


C3 Objectives and Estimands
Primary estimands are adjusted pathway-depression associations and pathway-prioritization signals derived from the joint pattern of upstream A-path support and downstream B-path evidence.
For mediator \(M_j\), the endpoint model is
\[
\operatorname{logit}\left\{P(Y_i^H=1 \mid S_{0-5}, S_{6-12}, M_1,\ldots,M_7, C)\right\}
=
\beta_0 + \beta_1^{\top}S_{0-5} + \beta_2^{\top}S_{6-12} + \sum_{j=1}^{7}\theta_j M_j + \beta_3^{\top} C,
\]
where \(S_{0-5}\) and \(S_{6-12}\) denote childhood trajectory-class representations, \(M_j\) denotes a candidate adolescent pathway domain, and \(C\) denotes fixed covariates. The primary parameter of interest for each pathway domain is \(\theta_j\), interpreted as the pathway-specific association with the harmonized depression endpoint after conditioning on transition history and the fixed covariate set.
Secondary estimands include A-path support hierarchy, nested-adjustment attenuation patterns, and ranking stability across model families. These secondary estimands will be used to decide which pathway domains warrant promotion into later mediation-focused analyses.


C4 Mediator Definitions and Coding
The current empirically testable adolescent pathway block comprises seven harmonized domains:

alcohol use;
ever having had sexual intercourse;
food insecurity;
lack of government grant receipt;
violence exposure;
low social support;
school disengagement.

These variables are treated as candidate pathway indicators rather than as already-identified formal mediators. They are harmonized from adolescent data collected after the childhood exposure windows and before the harmonized depression endpoint, with fixed covariate adjustment anchored on sex, age, rural residence, and calendar year (Marlow et al. 2023; I. Fortier et al. 2017; Chilver et al. 2023; Mthiyane et al. 2025).


C5 Modeling Strategy
Objective 4 carries forward transition-based exposure history rather than using isolated class snapshots. The childhood transition backbone is written as
\[
P(S_{0-5}, S_{6-12}, U^{(0-5)}, U^{(6-12)})
=
P(S_{0-5})
\times
P(S_{6-12} \mid S_{0-5})
\times
\prod_t P(U_t^{(0-5)} \mid S_{0-5})
\times
\prod_t P(U_t^{(6-12)} \mid S_{6-12}),
\]
with posterior class uncertainty carried into downstream screening rather than resolved through deterministic single-class assignment (Vermunt 2010; Asparouhov and Muthén 2014; Zyberaj, Bakaç, and Seibel 2022).
Within that transition framework, three analytic layers are prespecified:

A-path screening of pathway-variable probabilities as functions of childhood transition history and fixed covariates;
B-path models relating pathway domains to the harmonized depression endpoint after adjustment for transition history and fixed covariates;
jointly adjusted pathway models used to rank candidate pathway domains rather than to claim identified indirect effects.

All downstream regression stages use posterior-probability-weighted logistic models with participant-level cluster-robust standard errors. The active model-family set is aligned to Objective 3: ULWOM, UHSES, JPA, and JMAES.


C6 Mediator Prioritization and Phenotype Planning
Pathway prioritization for Objective 5 will be based on the joint pattern of:

A-path support across pre-specified primary and secondary hypothesis tiers;
B-path stability across the four active model families;
attenuation under nested fixed-covariate adjustment;
substantive plausibility in relation to the broader co-created pathway ecology.

Within that framework, food insecurity, violence exposure, and ever having had sexual intercourse are treated as high-interest candidate domains for close scrutiny because they are strongly motivated by the current substantive framework and the pathway-screening design. Alcohol use, lack of government grant receipt, low social support, and school disengagement remain part of the full evaluated pathway block and will be assessed under the same prioritization rules rather than excluded a priori (Duby et al. 2022; S. Mkhize et al. 2022).


C7 Sensitivity and Robustness
Objective 4 places particular emphasis on nested fixed-covariate adjustment. Sensitivity analyses will assess whether attenuation of pathway-depression associations is driven primarily by age, calendar year, rural residence, or combinations of these factors. This reflects the design assumption that developmental timing and secular period are part of the data-generating structure rather than optional nuisance adjustments.
Additional robustness layers include:

pathway ranking by pre-specified primary and secondary A-path hypothesis tiers rather than nominal significance alone (Benjamini and Hochberg 1995);
complete-case main analyses with targeted multiple-imputation diagnostics for maternal baseline variables (White, Royston, and Wood 2011; Sterne et al. 2009);
interaction reviews for selected pathway combinations, especially violence exposure with school disengagement;
reporting aligned to STROBE and AGReMA guidance (Elm et al. 2007; Moreno-Betancur et al. 2021).

These robustness checks reinforce that the current objective is prioritization for later mediation work, not a claim of identified direct and indirect effects (Imai, Keele, and Yamamoto 2010; Valeri and VanderWeele 2013; T. J. VanderWeele et al. 2016).


C8 Planned Outputs and Reporting
Planned outputs include:

A-path hierarchy tables by hypothesis tier and model family;
B-path association tables for the seven pathway domains;
nested-adjustment attenuation reviews showing the effect of age, rural residence, and calendar year;
a transparent shortlist of pathway domains prioritized for Objective 5;
contextual summaries linking the measured pathway block to the broader co-created pathway inventory.

section: Supplementary: Glossary of Symbols and Parameters
text:
Supplementary: Glossary of Symbols and Parameters
This glossary consolidates the core notation used in Sections A-C. Equation-specific parameter definitions remain embedded in the relevant sections.

Objective 2 Glossary

\(i\): participant index.
\(j\): item index.
\(k\): ordered-category threshold index for item \(j\).
\(Y_i^{PHQ}\): PHQ-referenced probable-depression indicator.
\(Y_i^H\): harmonized depression indicator.
\(\hat{\theta}_i\): participant-specific estimated latent depression score.
\(\tau^\ast\): selected latent threshold used to classify \(Y_i^H\).
\(\Delta_p\): prevalence drift between harmonized and PHQ-referenced endpoint prevalence.
\(\mathbf{x}_i\): vector of observed symptom item responses.
\(\boldsymbol{\lambda}\): factor-loading parameters.
\(\boldsymbol{\tau}\): CFA item-threshold parameters.
\(a_j\): GRM discrimination parameter for item \(j\).
\(b_{jk}\): GRM category-threshold parameter for item \(j\) at threshold \(k\).



Objective 3 Glossary

\(i\): participant index.
\(t\): developmental-time index within a childhood window.
\(c, h\): latent-class indices.
\(K\): total number of retained or candidate classes.
\(E_{it}\): observed exposure value for participant \(i\) at time \(t\).
\(C_i\): latent trajectory class membership.
\(\pi_c\): marginal class proportion for class \(c\).
\(Y_i^H\): harmonized depression outcome from Objective 2.
\(c_{ref}\): reference class for class-contrast models.
\(\eta_{0c}, \eta_{1c}, \eta_{2c}\): class-specific intercept and slope parameters.
\(\varepsilon_{it}\): residual term for participant \(i\) at time \(t\).
\(\sigma_c^2\): class-specific residual variance.
\(\alpha_c\): multinomial logit intercept governing class-membership probability.
\(\beta_c\): class-effect coefficient for class \(c\) relative to \(c_{ref}\).



Objective 4 Glossary

\(S_{0-5}\): childhood trajectory representation for the 0-5 year window.
\(S_{6-12}\): childhood trajectory representation for the 6-12 year window.
\(U^{(0-5)}, U^{(6-12)}\): observed childhood indicators contributing to each latent transition block.
\(M_j\): candidate adolescent pathway variable \(j\).
\(Y_i^H\): harmonized depression outcome.
\(C\): fixed covariate set used in the pathway-screening models.
\(\theta_j\): pathway-specific association parameter for mediator \(j\).
\(\beta_1, \beta_2\): transition-history effect vectors for the two childhood windows.
\(\beta_3\): fixed-covariate effect vector.

title: Project Metadata
text:
Study Design Extract
The CO-LUMINATE linked dataset connects individual-level mental health outcomes from cohort studies to household-level longitudinal HDSS records. The objective is to estimate longitudinal direct and mediation relationships between social determinants and depressive symptoms among youth aged 13-24.
The analytical design combines:

HDSS longitudinal household exposure histories.
Cohort-based depression outcomes measured with SSQ-14 and PHQ-9.
Linkage across five cohorts nested within AHRI HDSS.

title: Objective 5: Causal Mediation

title: Objective 4: Behavioral Mediators
text:
The childhood social-determinant work in CO-LUMINATE identified distinct patterns of early and middle-childhood adversity. The next question was more practical: through which adolescent conditions do those childhood histories appear to connect most clearly to later depressive symptoms?
This objective therefore focused on pathway screening rather than on final causal decomposition. The aim was to identify which adolescent pathway domains were coherent enough, consistent enough, and well enough represented in the harmonized data to deserve priority in later mediation analyses.

title: Objective 4: Behavioral Mediators
text:
The childhood social-determinant work in CO-LUMINATE identified distinct patterns of early and middle-childhood adversity. The next question was more practical: through which adolescent conditions do those childhood histories appear to connect most clearly to later depressive symptoms?
This objective therefore focused on pathway screening rather than on final causal decomposition. The aim was to identify which adolescent pathway domains were coherent enough, consistent enough, and well enough represented in the harmonized data to deserve priority in later mediation analyses.

title: Objective 4: Behavioral Mediators
text:
What Was Screened
The current retained screening analysis used three childhood exposure-model families:

lived without mother
household socioeconomic status
joint maternal absence and socioeconomic status

Across those model families, the screening stage evaluated seven adolescent pathway domains:

food insecurity
violence exposure
sexual activity
alcohol use
lack of government grant receipt
low social support
school disengagement

These domains were screened in two ways:

upstream, by asking whether childhood social-determinant histories were associated with the pathway domain
downstream, by asking whether the pathway domain remained associated with adolescent depressive symptoms after adjustment for exposure history and fixed contextual covariates


This page is a public summary of the pathway-screening stage. The internal analytical report contains fuller technical detail, fixed-covariate sensitivity review, and the JMAES imputation specification checks.

title: Objective 4: Behavioral Mediators
text:
Screening Framework
The figure below shows the broad logic of the screening stage. Childhood social-determinant histories are treated as the upstream exposure block, the seven screened adolescent pathway domains sit in the middle, and harmonized adolescent depressive symptoms are the endpoint.

title: Objective 4: Behavioral Mediators
text:
What We Found
The strongest overall pathway signal came from food insecurity. It combined the clearest evidence that childhood adversity histories were linked to the pathway itself with the clearest evidence that the pathway was linked to adolescent depressive symptoms.
Violence exposure was the next strongest candidate. Its upstream patterning was narrower than food insecurity, but its downstream association with depressive symptoms remained strong enough to keep it central in the interpretation.
Sexual activity remained relevant as a supporting pathway. Its downstream association was visible, but its upstream support was more limited, so it was retained more cautiously than the two leading domains.
Alcohol use and lack of government grant receipt showed weaker or less stable support in the retained screening set. Low social support and school disengagement were also screened and reported, but neither showed enough upstream or downstream support to enter the core prioritised set.

  
  Figure. Pathway evidence summary across the strict restricted screening set. Panel A shows supported upstream contrasts out of the total prespecified contrasts for each screened pathway and exposure-model family. Panel B shows the share of model families with 95% confidence intervals excluding the null, with point size tracking the median adjusted odds ratio across families. Panel C shows the pathways taken forward for interpretation based on combined upstream patterning and downstream support, while the upper panels retain the lower-priority screened domains as part of the full screening record.

title: Objective 4: Behavioral Mediators
text:
Main Recommendation
The current recommendation is:

carry food insecurity forward as the clearest candidate pathway for formal mediation follow-up
retain violence exposure as the next strongest candidate pathway
keep sexual activity as a supporting pathway domain
treat alcohol use and lack of government grant receipt more cautiously or reserve them for extended analyses

Internal sensitivity checks on the strict JMAES branch did not materially change that ordering, so the pathway priorities shown here remain the preferred summary for broad review.

title: Objective 4: Behavioral Mediators
text:
What This Means For CO-LUMINATE
For the broader CO-LUMINATE program, this work does not claim that a single pathway explains the relationship between childhood adversity and adolescent depression. Instead, it identifies where the current harmonized data give the clearest and most credible signals.
Taken together, the current evidence points to food insecurity and violence exposure as the strongest practical targets for the next-stage mediation work, while also showing that some pathway indicators are better interpreted as partial markers of a wider social ecology rather than as complete standalone mechanisms.

Co-Creating Knowledge On The Longitudinal Direct And Mediation Relationships Between Youth Mental Health And Social Stress Exposures







CO-LUMINATE investigates how childhood and adolescent social stress exposures shape depression risk over time among South African youth aged 13-24, using a longitudinally linked dataset nested within the AHRI Health and Demographic Surveillance System (HDSS). The study integrates four connected analytical components: depression endpoint harmonization, social determinant trajectory modeling, behavioral mediator assessment, and causal mediation analysis, with Youth Co-Creators guiding interpretation and context-sensitive framing so that findings remain scientifically rigorous, locally grounded, and policy-relevant.

title: CO-LUMINATE: Co-Creating Knowledge On The Longitudinal Direct And Mediation Relationships Between Youth Mental Health And Social Stress Exposures
text:
Project Overview
CO-LUMINATE leverages the Africa Health Research Institute (AHRI) Health and Demographic Surveillance System (HDSS) to create a longitudinal linked dataset of 5,279 adolescents and young people. The dataset supports analysis of how social determinants of mental health, including socioeconomic status (SES) and caregiver co-residency, relate to depressive symptoms over time.
The project also uses a structured co-creation model with 11 Youth Co-Creators (YCC), who contribute meaningful thought leadership to refine, guide, and nuance the research process and interpretation.

title: CO-LUMINATE: Co-Creating Knowledge On The Longitudinal Direct And Mediation Relationships Between Youth Mental Health And Social Stress Exposures

Eleven YCC members provide co-created input to strengthen relevance, interpretation, and context-sensitive framing of findings, and to guide analytical pathways.







3


Secondary Data Analysis
Longitudinal analyses evaluate relationships between social stress exposures (including SES and caregiver co-residency) and symptoms of depression.

title: Objective 2: Depression Harmonization
text:
CO-LUMINATE uses more than one mental-health instrument across studies and cohorts. The Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) is a conventional depression-focused reference measure, while the Shona Symptom Questionnaire captures a broader common mental distress space using culturally grounded binary items. Because those tools differ in content, response format, and construct emphasis, raw scores and raw cutoffs cannot be assumed to mean the same thing across instruments.
This psychometric work therefore asked a practical question: can we define a defensible depression-oriented endpoint that preserves the PHQ-9 benchmark while still allowing transport into cohorts where only SSQ items are available?

title: Objective 2: Depression Harmonization
text:
CO-LUMINATE uses more than one mental-health instrument across studies and cohorts. The Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9) is a conventional depression-focused reference measure, while the Shona Symptom Questionnaire captures a broader common mental distress space using culturally grounded binary items. Because those tools differ in content, response format, and construct emphasis, raw scores and raw cutoffs cannot be assumed to mean the same thing across instruments.
This psychometric work therefore asked a practical question: can we define a defensible depression-oriented endpoint that preserves the PHQ-9 benchmark while still allowing transport into cohorts where only SSQ items are available?

title: Objective 2: Depression Harmonization
text:
Data And Instruments
The harmonization-ready development sample comprised 843 adolescents and young adults with concurrent baseline SSQ and PHQ measurement. The work compared four instrument configurations:

the PHQ-9 as the depression-oriented reference benchmark
the original SSQ-14
a theory-refined SSQ-10 focused on depressive content
the shorter SSQ-08 subset

The central goal was not only to find a stable factor structure, but to identify which SSQ form best preserved a depression-focused latent trait suitable for later harmonization and downstream analysis.

title: Objective 2: Depression Harmonization
text:
What We Found
The main structural result was that the PHQ-9 remained a defensible depression-oriented reference, while the SSQ-10 emerged as the strongest retained local instrument for depression-focused harmonization. The full SSQ-14 carried broader common-distress content than was ideal for a transportable depression endpoint, and the SSQ-08 discarded too much of the more stable depressive core.

  

The retained SSQ-10 solution was analytically attractive because it preserved the strongest depressive content while avoiding the broader, more weakly targeted distress content that made the SSQ-14 harder to use as a depression endpoint. That made the SSQ-10 the most credible local instrument to carry into shared-scale harmonization.

title: Objective 2: Depression Harmonization
text:
Harmonization And Thresholding
The thresholding problem turned out to be as important as the retained instrument. Naive ROC-balanced SSQ cutoffs performed reasonably in discrimination terms, but they materially inflated the implied burden of depression relative to the PHQ-9 benchmark. In other words, a rule could look statistically appealing while still producing a public-health story that was too liberal to be credible for transport.
The more defensible solution came from Item Response Theory calibration. By placing PHQ-9 and SSQ-10 items on a shared latent scale, the analysis supported a clinically interpretable thresholding strategy tied back to the PHQ-9 reference. For cohorts where PHQ items are unavailable, the fixed-parameter SSQ-only scoring engine provided the most practical transport route.

  

The retained threshold strategy is therefore conservative by design. It favors a transportable SSQ-only engine that keeps estimated case burden close to the PHQ-9 benchmark rather than maximizing sensitivity at the cost of implausibly high prevalence.

title: Objective 2: Depression Harmonization
text:
Main Recommendation
The current recommendation is:

retain the SSQ-10 as the local depression-focused instrument
retain the joint PHQ-plus-SSQ IRT model as the harmonization reference standard
avoid naive raw-score thresholding as the main interpretation
carry forward the fixed-parameter SSQ-only scoring engine for cohorts without PHQ items

This combination provides the best balance between conceptual coherence, statistical defensibility, and practical transport into later developmental and causal analyses.

title: Objective 2: Depression Harmonization
text:
What This Means For CO-LUMINATE
For the broader CO-LUMINATE program, this work does not simply choose a questionnaire. It defines how depression should be represented when studies rely on different instruments. That matters for burden estimation, subgroup comparison, and any later work that treats depression as an outcome, a screening construct, or part of a harmonized longitudinal endpoint.
Taken together, these results indicate that the SSQ-10 can serve as the local bridge to a defensible depression-oriented endpoint, but only when interpreted through the harmonized thresholding strategy rather than through a raw SSQ cutoff carried over uncritically from another context.

title: Objective 1: Youth Co-Creation
text:
Abstract
This deliverable captures the youth co-creation workstream that informs hypothesis generation, lay DAG development, interpretation, and dissemination across the CO-LUMINATE study. In the private rebuild, the work is organized in two linked phases:

a pre-secondary analysis phase that develops a youth-informed pathway inventory and conceptual framing before formal modeling
a post-secondary analysis phase that uses youth interpretation to contextualize findings and strengthen stakeholder-facing communication

Together, these phases ensure that the technical analytical sequence remains locally grounded without turning the co-creation workstream into a separate modeled dataset.

title: Objective 1: Youth Co-Creation
text:
Keywords
youth co-creation; Youth Advisory Group; lay DAG; pathway inventory; interpretation workshop; dissemination

title: Objective 1: Youth Co-Creation
text:
Pre-Secondary Analysis
The pre-secondary analysis phase establishes the interpretive foundations for the later quantitative work. Following an inception workshop, Youth Advisory Group members contribute photo diaries, group-based narrative building, and a co-developed lay DAG describing how everyday social stressors may shape emotional distress among young people in the AHRI surveillance setting.
This phase contributes a contextual pathway inventory rather than a separate statistical dataset. The inventory identifies locally salient domains that help explain how structural disadvantage may become embodied as distress, including food strain, unsafe home and community environments, substance-related risk, school adversity, weak social connectedness, family separation, service-access barriers, and spiritual or cultural support.
That inventory then provides contextual grounding for the later behavioral mediator analysis. It helps justify why certain adolescent pathway variables were prioritized, clarifies what those measured variables do and do not capture, and keeps interpretation anchored in adolescents’ own accounts of how disadvantage becomes distress.

Full Pathway Inventory
The full contextual pathway inventory is shown below so the entire youth-informed pathway landscape is visible on this page rather than only a shortened subset.





Inventory domain
Inventory support
Contribution to later pathway screening
Current analytical capture




Food insecurity and material deprivation
Repeated pathways link poverty, crop loss, theft, mine closure, and household financial strain to food stress, worry, and poor mental health.
Provides strong local support for food insecurity as a proximal adolescent pathway domain and for interpreting it as one indicator of broader material deprivation.
Directly operationalized as food insecurity.


Violence exposure and unsafe environments
Inventory pathways repeatedly connect domestic violence, unsafe community spaces, taverns, crime, and substance-related insecurity to distress and poor mental health.
Provides strong local support for violence exposure as a pathway domain and for interpreting it as part of a wider insecurity ecology.
Directly operationalized as violence exposure.


Alcohol use and substance-related risk
Inventory pathways connect unsafe school and community environments, peer influence, financial strain, and substance use to emotional distress and poor mental health.
Provides direct contextual support for alcohol use as one observable component of broader substance-related and risk-exposure pathways.
Directly operationalized as alcohol use.


Sexual debut and broader adolescent risk ecology
Inventory pathways describe truancy, unsafe spaces, peer environments, and adolescent risk behaviours that may cluster around early sexual exposure and emotional strain.
Provides indirect contextual support for sexual debut while indicating that sexual debut captures only one slice of a wider adolescent risk ecology.
Partially operationalized as ever having had sexual intercourse.


Social protection and government grant conditions
Inventory pathways describe poverty, mine closure, reliance on grants, and the ambivalent social meaning of grant dependence, including stigma and eroded self-esteem.
Provides cautious support for social protection conditions as a pathway domain while indicating that grant-related experiences may be both protective and strained.
Partially operationalized as lack of government grant receipt.


School climate and educational pathway conditions
Inventory pathways highlight lack of school fencing, physical punishment, unsafe school environments, and early learning opportunities.
Shows that important school-related pathways are locally salient but only partly captured by the current pathway block.
Not directly harmonized in the present manuscript.


Family separation and migration-related stress
Inventory pathways connect migration for work and family separation to loneliness, insecurity, stress, and poor mental health.
Shows that migration-linked family separation is locally salient but outside the measured pathway block.
Not directly harmonized in the present manuscript.


Housing quality and water insecurity
Inventory pathways connect poor housing, water scarcity, hygiene constraints, bullying, and shame to emotional distress.
Shows that housing and water conditions form part of the broader pathway ecology but are not represented in the current analytic dataset.
Not directly harmonized in the present manuscript.


Social connectedness and community inclusion or exclusion
Inventory pathways describe community cohesion, exclusion from shared resources, low social connectedness, fear of stigma, and reduced help-seeking.
Shows that social-capital and exclusion pathways remain important contextual processes beyond the present measured variables.
Not directly harmonized in the present manuscript.


Service access, care barriers, and stigma
Inventory pathways describe long clinic queues, stigmatising treatment, transport barriers, and unmet care needs.
Shows that service-access and clinic-stigma pathways are locally salient but not tested in the present empirical model.
Not directly harmonized in the present manuscript.


Religion, spirituality, and cultural support or strain
Inventory pathways include church counselling, prayer, rituals, fear of witchcraft, and conflicting belief systems.
Shows that spiritual and cultural resources or stressors shape adolescent mental-health pathways but fall outside the current harmonized variables.
Not directly harmonized in the present manuscript.


Environmental degradation and climate stress
Inventory pathways link pollution, mine-related land damage, dumping, extreme weather, and erosion to fear, grief, and mental distress.
Shows that environmental and climate-linked pathways are important contextual mechanisms but remain unmeasured in the current analysis.
Not directly harmonized in the present manuscript.





In practical terms, the co-created pathway inventory strengthens the later pathway-screening work in two ways. First, it supports the selection of policy-relevant pathway domains that can be tested in the harmonized dataset. Second, it documents the broader pathway ecology that remains outside the measured variables, which is important for interpreting later findings with appropriate restraint.


Secondary Data Constraints
The inventory also makes an important secondary-data limitation visible. Even after careful harmonization, the measured adolescent pathway block captures only part of the broader pathway ecology described by young people. Several locally salient domains, including housing and water insecurity, migration-related family separation, service-access barriers, and spiritual or cultural support, are not directly measured in the current harmonized dataset.
This matters because the available indicators were assembled retrospectively across different source cohorts rather than designed as a single shared pathway instrument from the outset. As a result, a variable labelled as food insecurity, social support, school disengagement, or violence exposure may preserve the broad substantive domain while still differing in wording, response structure, timeframe, or local meaning across contributing studies.
The co-created pathway inventory therefore serves two purposes at once: it helps identify which measured pathway domains are locally grounded enough to carry forward into later analysis, and it clarifies where the secondary-data platform remains incomplete. This is why the measured pathway block should be understood as a constrained but policy-relevant subset of a wider adolescent pathway system, not as an exhaustive map of all relevant mechanisms.

title: Objective 1: Youth Co-Creation
text:
Post-Secondary Analysis
The post-secondary analysis phase begins after the main quantitative results are available. In this phase, Youth Advisory Group members engage with the emerging findings through structured interpretation and reflection workshops.
This workstream is intended to:

refine the explanatory narrative around the quantitative findings
identify where technical results align with, simplify, or miss lived pathway experience
support dissemination materials that are legible to community and stakeholder audiences
maintain context-sensitive framing when the study moves from analysis outputs to interpretation and communication

This post-secondary phase does not replace the statistical analyses. Instead, it strengthens the interpretive bridge between the technical results and the broader community-facing meaning of those results.

title: Project Proposal
text: CO-LUMINATE investigates how social stress exposures shape depressive symptoms among young people in rural South Africa over time. The work links cohort mental health outcomes with AHRI HDSS household trajectories to support longitudinal and mediation-focused analysis.

title: Project Proposal
text: CO-LUMINATE investigates how social stress exposures shape depressive symptoms among young people in rural South Africa over time. The work links cohort mental health outcomes with AHRI HDSS household trajectories to support longitudinal and mediation-focused analysis.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Scope

Population focus: adolescents and young adults aged 13-24 years.
Data structure: linked longitudinal cohort and HDSS records.
Core domains: socioeconomic status, food insecurity, caregiver co-residency, and related social stress exposures.
Outcome focus: symptoms of depression measured repeatedly over time.
Co-creation: youth partners contribute to interpretation and relevance framing.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Study Objectives (Full Statement)

Objective 1: Youth Co-Creation. Co-create a shared understanding of how social determinants shape young people’s mental health through a Youth Advisory Group, and integrate youth-led input into hypothesis generation, lay DAG development, interpretation, and dissemination framing.
Objective 2: Depression Harmonization. Describe prevalence of depression in the CO-LUMINATE dataset by harmonizing depression measures across cohorts (including SSQ and PHQ-9 sources) and deriving transportable depression outcomes for longitudinal analysis.
Objective 3: Social Determinant Trajectories. Characterize social determinant trajectories across childhood windows, including early childhood (0-5 years) and later childhood (6-12 years), and identify exposure-pattern pathways linked to adolescent depression outcomes.
Objective 4: Behavioral Mediators. Examine behavioral mediators, including migration, substance use, and sexual behaviour, as pathway mechanisms linking social determinants to depressive symptoms, and prioritize mediator structures for downstream causal modeling.
Objective 5: Causal Mediation. Estimate direct and mediated effects of key social determinants on depression among young people using longitudinal causal mediation methods and context-sensitive interpretation informed by the youth co-creation workstream.

title: Project Proposal
text:
Planned Deliverables

Curated linked analysis datasets with supporting metadata and documentation.
Reproducible statistical workflows, technical reports, and presentation outputs.
Evidence summaries for research, policy, and stakeholder engagement.
Co-created interpretation products to strengthen contextual validity and uptake.
